meta.description: Rules and weights for scoring any action against the Stoic virtue framework. Used by both the scoring API (P6-P7) and the indifferents ranking engine (P5). Derived from Cicero De Officiis Books 1 and 3, Epictetus Enchiridion, and Diogenes Laertius Lives 7.

scoring_model.name: Virtue-Weighted Composite Score
scoring_model.description: An action receives a score of 0-100 by assessing how fully it expresses each of the four cardinal virtues, weighted by their relative importance. A perfect sage score (100) is theoretically possible but practically unreachable.

virtue_weights.note: Weights reflect the Stoic view that wisdom (phronesis) is the master virtue from which all others derive. These can be adjusted as the model is validated against source texts.

score_range.note: Each individual virtue is also scored 0-100 before weighting is applied.

wisdom.description: Score 0-100 on how well the action reflects correct understanding of what is genuinely good vs indifferent
score_high_if: The agent acts from accurate understanding of what is in their control vs not
The agent correctly identifies the virtuous path rather than the merely pleasant or profitable one
The agent anticipates consequences without being enslaved to fear of them
The action reflects long-term reasoning rather than reactive impulse
score_low_if: The agent treats an indifferent (money, health, status) as if it were genuinely good
The action is driven by false belief — about what will actually help, or what is truly valuable
The agent fails to distinguish what is in their control from what is not
The action is purely reactive with no deliberation
wisdom.benchmark_question: Would a person with complete clarity about what is truly good — who knows that only virtue matters — take this action for this reason?

justice.description: Score 0-100 on how well the action serves others rightly and fulfils social duties
score_high_if: The action benefits others or serves the common good
The agent fulfils their duties to family, community, or humanity (oikeiosis — natural affection toward others)
The agent treats others as fellow rational beings deserving of respect
The agent is honest and fair in dealings with others
score_low_if: The action harms others or prioritises self at others' expense
The agent lies, deceives, or withholds information that others need
The agent fails in their social role or duty
The action ignores the impact on people beyond the immediate situation
justice.benchmark_question: Does this action treat every person affected as a fellow citizen of the cosmos, deserving what they are rightly owed?

courage.description: Score 0-100 on whether the agent acts rightly despite difficulty, discomfort, or apparent risk
score_high_if: The agent acts rightly when it would be easier or safer not to
The agent endures pain, loss, or social disapproval without abandoning virtue
The agent persists through difficulty in the service of what is right
The agent faces death, failure, or rejection without flinching from their duty
score_low_if: The agent avoids a right action because it is uncomfortable or risky
Fear of external consequences (reputation, pain, rejection) overrides what is right
The agent gives up when things become difficult
Recklessness — risk taken from passion or ego rather than virtue
courage.benchmark_question: Does the agent do what is right despite the cost, without being either paralysed by fear or reckless from passion?

temperance.description: Score 0-100 on how measured, orderly, and passion-free the action is
score_high_if: The action is measured and fitting — neither excessive nor deficient
The agent is not driven by craving, anger, fear, or grief (the four Stoic passions)
The action reflects considered choice rather than impulse
The agent takes what is appropriate without excess or compulsion
score_low_if: The action is driven by appetite, lust, anger, or craving
The agent acts excessively — too much, too intensely, or at the wrong time
The agent is ruled by the desire to avoid pain or gain pleasure
The action is impulsive, reactive, or emotionally driven
temperance.benchmark_question: Is the action completely free of passion — driven by reason and appropriate to the situation, without excess in any direction?

intention_over_outcome.principle: Stoic ethics is primarily concerned with the quality of the agent's intention and reasoning, not the outcome. An agent who acts virtuously but achieves a bad result is still scored near the top of their virtue range. An agent who achieves a good outcome through vicious means scores low.
intention_over_outcome.application: When scoring, assess what the agent intended and why — not just what happened. The question is: 'Was this the kind of action a person of good character would take, for the right reasons?'

dichotomy_filter.principle: Before scoring any virtue, check whether the action falls within the agent's prohairesis (sphere of control). Actions taken under genuine constraint (imprisonment, coercion) are assessed on how the agent responds to the constraint, not the constraint itself.
dichotomy_filter.application: If the agent had no real choice about the external situation, the score reflects how they responded within their sphere — their intention, attitude, and reasoning — not the external event.

conflict_resolution.description: When virtues appear to conflict (e.g. being just seems to require being courageous at personal cost), Cicero De Officiis Book 3 provides the resolution framework.

rules.resolution: Virtue always wins. If achieving a preferred indifferent (wealth, health, reputation) requires a vice, refuse the indifferent. Score the virtuous refusal at 100.
Prioritise duties in this order: (1) to the gods/rational cosmos, (2) to universal humanity, (3) to country, (4) to parents/family, (5) to others. A higher duty always supersedes a lower one.

The Stoics held that lying is never virtuous. However, silence or redirection (not revealing harmful information without lying) may be permitted. Lying to prevent harm still scores low on justice and wisdom.

Actions taken purely for self-interest at others' expense score near zero on justice. However, proper self-care (health, development) that enables greater future virtue scores moderately on justice as it serves one's capacity for duty.
rules.source: Cicero De Officiis 1.7-8; Marcus Aurelius Meditations 5.8

indifferents_scoring_map.description: P5 — How to rank competing external goals (indifferents) for a user. A preferred indifferent is worth pursuing only to the degree it can be pursued virtuously. Use this map to advise which external goal to prioritise when multiple options exist.
indifferents_scoring_map.principle: Rank indifferents by: (1) how much the pursuit of them can be expressed through virtue, (2) how naturally aligned they are with the agent's current roles and duties (kathêkon), (3) how low the risk of attachment or excess.

ranking_algorithm.step_1: Check virtue_relevance scores in indifferents.json for each option
ranking_algorithm.step_2: Identify which option allows the greatest total virtue expression (sum of virtue_relevance * virtue_weight)
ranking_algorithm.step_3: Check whether the agent's current role (family, work, community) creates a specific duty toward any option
ranking_algorithm.step_4: Flag any option where 'common_misuse' risk is high — reduce its rank accordingly
ranking_algorithm.output: Ranked list with explanation of which option best serves virtue given the agent's situation

past_action_scoring.description: P6 — How to score a completed action submitted by a user.
input_fields: action_description: string — what did the agent do
context: string (optional) — relevant background (role, constraints, relationships involved)
intended_outcome: string (optional) — what the agent was trying to achieve
output_fields: reasoning: string — why each virtue was scored as it was
improvement_path: string — what a sage would have done differently
key_indifferents: array — which indifferents were at stake
strength: string — which virtue was most expressed
growth_area: string — which virtue needs most development

prospective_action_scoring.description: P7 — How to advise on a proposed action before it is taken.
input_fields: proposed_action: string — what the agent is considering doing
alternatives: array (optional) — other options being considered
concerns: string (optional) — what the agent is worried about
output_fields: recommended_action: string — what the sage path recommends
virtue_alignment_scores: object — projected score for each option if alternatives provided
key_considerations: array — the most important virtue considerations
indifferents_at_stake: array — what externals are involved and how to relate to them
sage_framing: string — how a sage would think about this situation
caution: string (optional) — any passion or false belief likely to distort judgement

sage.label: Sage (Sophos)
sage.description: Near-perfect virtue expression across all four virtues simultaneously.

progressing.label: Progressing (Prokoptos)
progressing.description: Strong virtue expression with clear awareness and consistent effort.

aware.label: Aware
aware.description: Some virtue expression but still partly ruled by passion or false opinion.

misaligned.label: Misaligned
misaligned.description: Primarily ruled by externals and passion. Limited engagement with virtue.

contrary.label: Contrary to Nature
contrary.description: Actions actively opposed to virtue — vicious rather than merely unaware.
